zzpower 1.0.0

zzpower: Interactive Power Analysis Calculator for Clinical Trials

Source: R/zzpower-package.R
zzpower-package.Rd

The zzpower package provides an interactive 'Shiny' application covering eleven statistical tests via a registry/plugin architecture: two-group, paired, and one-sample t-tests; two-proportion comparisons; Pearson correlation; log-rank survival; Fisher's exact test; Cochran-Armitage trend; one-way ANOVA; McNemar's test for paired proportions; and a basic linear mixed-model power calculation. Each test supports multiple effect-size specifications, design parameters (alpha, allocation, dropout), and downloadable reports.

Details

A 'Shiny' application for power analysis and sample size calculations across a catalogue of statistical tests common in clinical trial design.

Key Features

  • Eleven statistical tests via the get_power_test_registry registry, each rendered through a generic UI/server factory pair (create_generic_test_ui, create_generic_test_server)

  • Multiple effect-size methods per test (Cohen's d, h, f, hazard ratio, odds ratio, percent reduction, etc.)

  • Sample-size and power solve modes: compute power at a given N or N required for target power

  • Interactive power curves with reference lines and formatted result tables (DT)

  • Downloadable reports (text and HTML) summarising study design and power calculation

  • Design considerations: dropout, drop-in, allocation ratio, one-sided vs two-sided testing

Getting Started

To launch the interactive application, run:


library(zzpower)
launch_zzpower()

References

Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.

Schoenfeld, D. A. (1983). Sample-size formula for the proportional- hazards regression model. Biometrics, 39(2), 499-503.

Champely, S. (2020). pwr: Basic Functions for Power Analysis. R package.

See also

  • launch_zzpower for the interactive application

  • get_power_test_registry for the test registry

  • The pwr package for the underlying power-calculation primitives

Author

Ronald G. Thomas
